nativeRange.habitat: Coral reef caves, ledges, and crevices at depths of 1–60 m; tends to orient belly toward the substrate and has been observed swimming upside-down under overhangs

diet.notes: Has been documented accepting a variety of meaty fare in captivity, including brine shrimp, mysis shrimp, frozen plankton, and quality pellet or flake preparations. In the wild, has been observed feeding on ectoparasites of other fishes and small zooplankton.

avoidWith: other Royal Grammas (unless mated pair in larger tank)
Royal Dottyback and similar-looking purple fish

imageCandidates.notes: Brian Gratwicke; 2403x1656. Tack-sharp classic side profile showing the diagnostic magenta-to-gold bicolor split, dark line through the eye, and black spot on the front of the dorsal fin; vibrant live specimen.
cello caruso-turiello; 2048x1365. Sharp in-situ reef shot, whole fish in frame; clear purple-to-orange bicolor, black dorsal spot and eye line all visible on a vivid live individual.

H. Zell; 4400x3168. Highest-resolution option, crisp full side profile with strong purple-to-yellow gradient and orange spotting; eye line visible. Dorsal black spot less prominent than the recommended pick.

summary: The Royal Gramma Basslet is a small, vividly bicolored reef fish from the Caribbean, widely regarded as beginner-friendly and reef-safe. It is generally best kept singly, as it tends to be territorial toward conspecifics and similarly colored species.
careNotes:
The Royal Gramma Basslet is generally considered one of the more straightforward saltwater fish to keep, making it a popular choice for reef aquariums. It tends to occupy mid-level swim zones close to rockwork, often retreating into caves and crevices. The species has been known to swim upside-down under ledges, which is entirely normal behavior.

Conspecific aggression is a notable concern. The Royal Gramma is generally best kept as a single specimen; if a mated pair is attempted, a larger system is advised — typically 50 gallons or more for a pair, or 100 gallons or more for a small group. The species has also been known to show aggression toward other fish that share a similar purple-and-yellow coloration, such as the Royal Dottyback (Pictichromis paccagnellae).

The Royal Gramma has been reported to be a capable jumper, so a tightly fitted lid is generally advised. Providing ample rockwork with caves and overhangs gives the fish places to retreat — specimens tend to be shy when first introduced and may take some time before venturing regularly into open water.
breedingNotes: The Royal Gramma has been reported to breed readily in captivity. Males establish nest sites in small holes and crevices, and females deposit approximately 5–40 eggs per spawning event. The eggs are typically guarded by the male and have been reported to hatch within 5–7 days under aquarium conditions. Raising the fry is generally considered moderately challenging, primarily due to the small size of the larvae.

fish.breedingNotes: Has been reported to breed readily in captivity. Males build nests in crevices and guard the eggs until hatching. The fry are small, and raising them is generally considered moderately difficult.
